record.bridgyOriginalText:
In April, GitHub announced that it was moving subscribers from
request-based billing to a
usage-based model for its AI-powered Copilot service.
As that new pricing model goes into effect today,
many GitHub Copilot users are reporting some extreme sticker shock
as they realize just how quickly their previous “normal” usage is burning through their newly limited monthly allotment of AI credits.

Across social media and forums,
many Copilot users are sharing personal statistics
showing how just a few hours of AI usage can now account for a large chunk of their new monthly subscription caps.
For some users, it reportedly took less than a day to use up a month’s usage quota.
That’s a big change from previous months,
when GitHub Copilot subscribers were allocated a certain number of “requests” and “premium requests”
based on their payment tier.
GitHub said that the old system meant that “a quick chat question and a multi-hour autonomous coding session [could] cost the user the same amount,”
forcing Copilot itself to “absorb much of the escalating inference cost behind that usage.” 
Indeed, some Copilot users have been sharing estimates from GitHub’s own tools showing that their previous monthly usage would rack up bills in the thousands of dollars under the new pricing plan
https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/06/ai-costs-how-much-github-copilot-users-react-to-new-usage-based-pricing-system/

Rob Sand, the best-known Democrat in Iowa,
appears on podcasts to discuss his love of hunting, begins rallies by having the audience sing America the Beautiful and has a tendency to criticize the country’s two-party political system.

Now, Sand is running to lead a state that Republicans have come to dominate under Donald Trump,
and Democrats believe his candidacy for governor could be the breakthrough needed to win key Iowa offices in the November midterm elections.

With Trump’s approval ratings deep underwater,
gas prices high and historical political trends favoringthe party out of power,
Democrats this year are considering a comeback in Iowa,
putting the state at the center of their campaigns to win back control of both the US House of Representatives and the Senate.

On Tuesday, voters will cast ballots in primary elections that set the stage for months of what is likely to be fevered campaigning by candidates of both parties.

“I think this is a once-in-a-generation opportunity for us to be able to win here in Iowa.
I mean, this is a state that has completely hit the bottom,”
said Josh Turek, a state representative who is one of two Democrats vying to represent Iowa in the US Senate

record.bridgyOriginalText:
The conservative Californiacommunity of Huntington Beach -- known for banning the Pride flag from city property and fighting the state over pandemic and housing policy -- could soon be represented by a gay, Democratic congressman.

Robert Garcia, an incumbent two-term Democratic LGBTQ+ congressman, immigrant and Donald Trump critic,
is considered the leading contender in Tuesday’s primary race for the US House seat.

Thanks to a successful restricting effort that redrew California’s voting maps to favor Democrats,
Huntington Beach is now part of Garcia’s congressional district 42.

California’s redistricting effort, known as Proposition 50,
was overwhelmingly approved by voters in November.

Trump set off a wave of new mid-decade redistricting after urging Texas to adopt changes to favor Republicans during the midterm elections.
California soon followed, as have a number of other states, reshaping representation across the US.

【美国观察：GitHub Copilot 引入按量计费】

GitHub Copilot 开始尝试基于用量的计费系统，部分用户反馈其每月 AI 额度在短时间内被耗尽。这标志着 AI 编程工具从“订阅制”向“资源消耗制”转移，对重度依赖 Copilot 的 SDE 来说，开发成本不再固定，可能增加成本焦虑。同时预示着 AI 供应商正将算力成本压力传导至用户。后续关注 Cursor 等工具是否跟进。

来源：Ars Technica

【美国观察：特朗普介入黎以停火谈判】

Alphabet 拟融资 800 亿美元用于 AI 建设

谷歌母公司 Alphabet 正计划筹集 800 亿美元，以支持其庞大的 AI 基础设施建设。公司表示，企业对 AI 解决方案的需求极其旺盛，已超过了其现有供应能力。

分析：这标志着 AI 军备竞赛正进入“重资产”时代。不仅是算法的竞争，更是资本与电力、芯片、数据中心的全面角逐。对于投资者而言，AI 巨头已进入必须通过超大规模投入来维持领先地位的阶段，这也预示着其现金流压力将持续增大。

关注：观察其融资方式以及资本开支如何转化为实际的营收增长。

来源：TechCrunch

external.description: GitHubは2026年6月1日より、Copilotの料金体系をトークン消費に基づくGitHub AIクレジット制へ完全に移行しました。ProやBusinessといった各プランには月額料金に応じたクレジットが付与されます。Copilot Chatやエージェント機能、コードレビュー、CLIの利用はクレジットを消費しますが、インライン補完やNext Edit Suggestionsは引き続き無制限で利用可能です。 今回の変更により、フロンティアモデルを用いた高度なエージェントセッションや長時間のコード生成を行う開発者ほどクレジット消費が激しくなり、月間枠を短期間で使い切るリスクが指摘されています。クレジットが枯渇した場合、設定次第では追加料金が発生するか、主要機能が停止する仕組みです。GitHubはコスト管理のためにユーザーごとの予算上限設定や、低コストモデルの活用を推奨しています。特に自動化機能を多用するユーザーにとっては、利用形態に応じた柔軟な運用と予算監視が不可欠な状況となっています。

Intel introduces Xeon 6+ server processors, previews upcoming graphics cards
->SiliconANGLE | More on "Intel Xeon 6+ AI processors" at BigEarthData.ai

external.description: Intel Corp. today debuted a line of central processing units based on its latest manufacturing process and shared new details about its next-generation graphics cards. Both product families are geared toward artificial intelligence data centers. Intel is also prioritizing the AI infrastructure market with a new series of Ethernet chips that it announced in conjunction. The Xeon 6+ product series comprises six CPUs based on Intel’s Darkmont core design, which prioritizes power efficiency over performance. The most advanced chip in the lineup, the Xeon 6990E+, features 288 cores. They’re backed by an L3 cache that is several times larger than the one in Intel’s previous-generation CPU. The company says the processor provides 30% higher power efficiency and performance per thread than Advanced Micro Devices Inc.’s comparable Epyc 9965. The GCF Task Force unites 45 subnational governments across 11 countries, representing more than a third of the world’s tropical forests.The meeting brought together subnational governments, Indigenous Peoples and local communities, civil society, and the private sector around the theme “New Forest Economy for Climate Action: Territorial Development and Innovation.” At the meeting, Amazon Mining Watch was highlighted as a valuable tool in the fight against illegal gold mining across the Amazon basin . #aukus #defence #australia

external.description:
Former Australian environment minister Peter Garrett has announced his involvement in an independent inquiry examining the Aukus defence agreement, a major security partnership between Australia, the United Kingdom and the United States. The review has been initiated by a group of Labor Party veterans and other prominent public figures who argue that the project has not received sufficient public scrutiny despite its estimated cost of $368 billion and its long-term implications for Australia's defence policy.

In the Full Story podcast, Garrett discusses concerns surrounding the Aukus program, particularly the financial burden, strategic risks and Australia's increasingly close military relationship with the United States. Supporters of the inquiry believe that Australians deserve a transparent assessment of whether the agreement will genuinely improve national security or expose the country to greater risks.

The discussion highlights growing debate about the acquisition of nuclear-powered submarines, including questions about escalating costs, project delays and uncertainty over whether the submarines will ultimately be delivered as planned. Critics also warn that military facilities linked to the Aukus program could make Australia a more prominent target during international conflicts.

Garrett argues that the scale and significance of the agreement require a thorough and independent examination so that the public can better understand its consequences. The podcast explores whether Aukus represents a necessary investment in Australia's future security or whether it creates new vulnerabilities and strategic dependencies. The inquiry seeks to contribute to a broader national conversation about defence priorities, public accountability and Australia's role within an increasingly complex geopolitical environment.
external.title: Peter Garrett Backs Independent Inquiry into Aukus Costs, Risks and Strategic Consequences

Tentative U.S.–Iran agreement signals possible end to conflict and reopening of Strait of Hormuz

🤖 IA: It's not clickbait ✅
👥 Users: It's not clickbait ✅

#iranusrelations #straitofhormuz #middleeastconflict 

View full AI summary:

external.description:
Senior U.S. and Iranian officials reported Thursday that both countries have reached a tentative agreement aimed at ending months of armed conflict in the Middle East and restoring maritime access through the strategically critical Strait of Hormuz. The preliminary accord represents a significant diplomatic breakthrough after prolonged hostilities that disrupted regional stability and global energy transport routes. However, the agreement is not yet final and remains subject to approval from U.S. President Donald Trump and Iranian leadership.

According to officials familiar with the discussions, the deal outlines a framework intended to de-escalate military tensions and reopen vital shipping lanes that had been blocked or threatened during the conflict. The Strait of Hormuz, a crucial passageway for global oil shipments, has been a focal point of geopolitical tension, and its reopening is considered a key element for restoring economic stability in international markets.

Despite the optimism surrounding the tentative accord, significant uncertainties remain. Both governments are still reviewing the terms, and political approval is required before any formal implementation can proceed. U.S. officials emphasized that while progress has been made, the situation remains fluid and dependent on high-level decisions in Washington and Tehran.

In parallel diplomatic activity, envoys from Israel and Lebanon were scheduled to meet in Washington on Friday to address ongoing regional clashes that have further complicated efforts to reach a broader peace framework. These discussions highlight the interconnected nature of conflicts across the region and the challenges facing negotiators seeking a comprehensive settlement.

If finalized, the agreement could mark a major shift in Middle Eastern geopolitics, potentially reducing military confrontation and reopening critical trade routes. However, until both leaderships formally endorse the deal, the outcome remains uncertain.
external.title: Tentative U.S.–Iran agreement signals possible end to conflict and reopening of Strait of Hormuz

Jeff Colyer Ends 2026 GOP Governor Campaign After Trump Endorses Ty Masterson

🤖 IA: It's not clickbait ✅
👥 Users: It's not clickbait ✅

#kansas #governorrace #gop 

View full AI summary:

external.description: Former Kansas Governor Jeff Colyer officially ended his campaign for the Republican nomination for the 2026 gubernatorial race following President Donald Trump's endorsement of his rival, Senate President Ty Masterson. Colyer, who briefly served as governor after Sam Brownback’s resignation in 2018 and withdrew from the 2022 race for health reasons, stated that he would continue public service but would not seek the governorship in 2026. Masterson, endorsed by Trump on April 24, highlighted that the endorsement allowed him to choose a running mate, Lansing State Senator Jeff Klemp, based on qualifications rather than demographics. Other GOP candidates include Insurance Commissioner Vicki Schmidt with running mate Joe Newland, businessman Philip Sarnecki with Joy Eakins, marijuana activist Nick Reinecker with his wife Katy, Charlotte O’Hara with Michael Smith, and Stacy Rogers with Michelle Dombrosky. Candidates emphasized issues ranging from fiscal responsibility and property tax reform to political accountability and leadership for Kansans. The August primary will determine the GOP nominee for the November general election against incumbent Democratic Governor Laura Kelly. Colyer expressed gratitude to supporters and campaign staff, noting that the campaign was about advancing Kansas’ potential rather than personal ambition.
external.title: Jeff Colyer Ends 2026 GOP Governor Campaign After Trump Endorses Ty Masterson

Rachel Maddow Criticizes Trump's Planning and Execution of 250th Anniversary Events

🤖 IA: It's clickbait ⚠️
👥 Users: It's clickbait ⚠️

#trump #rachelmaddow #anniversarycelebrations 

View full AI summary:

external.description:
During an episode of MSNBC's 'MS NOW,' Rachel Maddow criticized President Donald Trump and his administration over a series of problems surrounding planned celebrations for the United States' 250th anniversary. Maddow began by discussing a controversy in Philadelphia involving memorials to enslaved people at the President's House. According to her account, local residents successfully challenged efforts to remove the memorials, but the National Park Service later restored only part of the site, leaving it visibly incomplete. Maddow argued that this unfinished restoration symbolized what she described as poorly executed anniversary events associated with Trump.

She highlighted several reported setbacks connected to the celebrations, including plans for entertainment events, promotional campaigns, and public performances. Maddow mocked a proposed event featuring a combat sports spectacle near the White House and criticized marketing materials connected to a planned race event in Washington, D.C., noting that merchandise bearing the phrase 'One Nation, One Race' was withdrawn after controversy.

Maddow also claimed that a planned anniversary concert faced difficulties after performers reportedly withdrew amid public criticism, leading Trump to suggest a greater personal role in the event. She compared these developments to issues she said surrounded Trump's presidential inaugurations, which she characterized as suffering from limited celebrity participation and organizational problems.

The segment further referenced other events that Maddow portrayed as unsuccessful, including a military parade associated with Trump's birthday and a conservative alternative to the Super Bowl halftime show. Throughout the broadcast, Maddow used satire and criticism to argue that the administration's anniversary celebrations reflected broader organizational shortcomings.
external.title: Rachel Maddow Criticizes Trump's Planning and Execution of 250th Anniversary Events

The best systems have redundancy. You wouldn't fly a commercial flight run by a single pilot, so why do we trust an AI workflow that can only fail in one place?

Architecture matters more than complexity. Watch this thread for thoughts on safety. [Link]

Instead of worrying about how many agent types exist, I focus on a 3-part flow: Knowledge (your docs), Decision (your mental models), and Action (your SOPs).

If you're starting out, build the Action Agent first. It's the path of least resistance.

This is entirely ludicrous, BTW, but let's look at the crazy town numbers as a start. 

If each Starship can launch 1x per day, assume 20% in refurb, you're looking at a fleet of 36 Starships. That is reasonable when compared to the number of 777 in service. 

Now let's look at fuel.

What does 10k Starship launches per year imply?

That is 27-28 launches per day. Let's assume ballistic launch on all 10k, with a 6-hour duration from propellant loading to landing. Musk claims he'll have 8 hour turn around on. Let's call it 18 hours, fully optimized, from landing to back on a pad.

The FAA has so far approved a potential 44 Starship launches a year. This IPO requires "thousands of launches" (p 9). Musk, on various things, has said he foresees 10k launches a year, which is thousands. This will require new launch sites/pads/atmospheric badness.  www.reuters.com/science/spac...
